name: Modular Monolith

forces: Future extraction to services ↔ avoiding premature distribution
x.structure: One deployable process internally partitioned into modules with explicit, enforced boundaries and contracts; modules communicate in-process but do not reach into each other’s internals or databases.
antiPatterns: Big ball of mud (no real boundaries)
x.scale: Whole application; a disciplined alternative to microservices.

def: A single deployable application that is internally divided into well-bounded, loosely-coupled modules — the modularity benefits of microservices without distributed-systems cost.
problem: Microservices add network, deployment, and consistency complexity that many systems do not need; an unstructured monolith decays into a big ball of mud.
mechanism: Enforce module boundaries within one process (separate packages/assemblies, explicit public contracts, per-module data ownership, dependency rules checked in CI). Modules can later be extracted into services if and when scaling demands it.
benefits: Clear path to extract services later
limits: Boundaries are convention — easy to erode without enforcement
failures: Boundaries decaying into a ball of mud
Modules sharing a database schema directly
whenNot: Independent scaling / deployment per component is essential
myths: "Monolith = unmaintainable" — a modular monolith is often the right default before microservices
